1: Pashhur son of Immer, the chief temple officer, represents the institutional religious establishment in its most direct confrontation with Jeremiah's prophetic ministry. The name Pashhur (possibly meaning "prosperity on every side") will be inverted into a curse-name (v3). Christologically, the religious official who arrests and beats the prophet prefigures Caiaphas and the Sanhedrin who arrest and try Jesus (Matthew 26:57-67). In both cases the institutional religious authority violently opposes the one YHWH sent.
2: "Pashhur had Jeremiah the prophet beaten and put in the stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate." The beating (hikkâ) and public humiliation (stocks, maḥpeket) of the prophet for speaking YHWH's word is the OT pattern of prophetic suffering. Christologically, Jesus's beating and public humiliation at the hands of the religious authorities (Matthew 27:26-31; John 19:1-3) is the fulfillment of this pattern. The prophet beaten for speaking the divine word becomes the type of the Son beaten for being the divine Word.
3: "The LORD has not named you Pashhur, but Magor-Missabib — Terror-on-Every-Side." The prophetic omen-name inversion: Pashhur's pleasant name is replaced by the terror-judgment name that Jeremiah himself has been applying to Judah (Jeremiah 6:25; Jeremiah 46:5). Christologically, the naming of the persecutor with the judgment-name he sought to silence recalls Jesus's own naming of opponents: "Brood of vipers" (Matthew 23:33), "Whitewashed tombs" (Matthew 23:27) — the one who is persecuted has the authority to rename his persecutors with the truth of their condition.
4: "I am going to make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends." The oracle against Pashhur turns the institutional persecutor's own security against him — the very power-structure he defended will be the instrument of his destruction. Christologically, this reversal anticipates the pattern of Acts: the Sanhedrin that condemned Jesus becomes the institution that loses its own members to the gospel (Acts 6:7: "a great many of the priests became obedient to the faith"). The persecutors of the prophet become, in many cases, recipients of the very word they tried to silence.
5: "I will also hand over all the wealth of this city... to their enemies." The comprehensive confiscation of Jerusalem's treasures to Babylon is the covenant-curse consequence of institutional unfaithfulness. Christologically, the stripping of institutional wealth and prestige echoes Jesus's prediction of Jerusalem's complete desolation (Matthew 24:2: "there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down"). The wealth that the temple establishment tried to protect by killing the prophet is precisely what is lost when the prophet is silenced.
6: "And you, Pashhur, with everyone in your household, will go into exile. You will go to Babylon, and there you will die." The personal exile-oracle against Pashhur extends to his entire household and concludes with death in exile — which is also the fate of the false prophets he supported. Christologically, the judgment pronounced on the persecutor is the OT form of Jesus's judgment-word against those who kill the prophets: "Truly, I say to you, all these things will come upon this generation" (Matthew 23:36). Condemnation follows the killers of prophets across generations.
7: "O LORD, you deceived me, and I let myself be deceived; you overpowered me and you won." The Sixth Confession opens with the most extreme accusation in the Psalter/Prophetic tradition against YHWH — the language of seduction/deception (pittîtanî, Piel of pātâ, used for erotic seduction in Exodus 22:16; Judges 14:15). Jeremiah accuses YHWH of luring him into the prophetic office and then leaving him to suffer its consequences. Christologically, this raw prophetic cry of abandonment is the OT form of the cry of dereliction: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46; Psalms 22:1). Jesus takes this cry further — not mere accusation but the actual experience of divine abandonment that Jeremiah only feared. Hebrews 5:7-8 ("in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears") applies this confessional pattern to Christ's passion prayer.
8: "Every time I speak, I have to cry out — 'Violence! Destruction!' — and the word of the LORD has become for me nothing but reproach and mockery all day long." The word of YHWH that Jeremiah cannot escape becomes the source of his reproach. Christologically, John 1:11 ("he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him") and Hebrews 13:13 ("let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured") identify Christ as the supreme bearer of reproach for speaking and being the divine word. The ḥerpâ (reproach, mockery) of v8 is the same ḥerpâ that Psalms 69:7-9, 20 identifies as the central suffering of the righteous sufferer — cited in the NT passion tradition (Romans 15:3; John 2:17).
9: "When I think, 'I will not mention him, I will speak in his name no more,' then there is in my heart something like a burning fire shut up in my bones." The burning-fire-in-the-bones is the most vivid description of prophetic compulsion in the OT — the word of YHWH is not an option Jeremiah can refuse, even when it costs him everything. Christologically, Hebrews 12:1-2 ("looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame") describes Christ's own movement through suffering by means of a fixed purpose that could not be extinguished. Jesus's statement in Luke 12:49 ("I came to cast fire on the earth, and would that it were already kindled!") echoes the burning-fire compulsion — the mission could not be held back. John 4:34 ("My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work") expresses the same irresistible forward movement.
10: "For I hear many people whispering, 'Terror on every side! Report him — let us report him!' All my trusted friends are waiting for me to slip." The betrayal by trusted friends (ʾănšê šĕlōmî, lit. men of my peace) anticipates Psalms 41:9 ("even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me") — cited by Jesus in John 13:18 for Judas's betrayal. The pattern of betrayal by trusted friends in Jeremiah 20:10 is the same pattern that Jesus identifies as the specific form his betrayal will take. The intimate betrayal is the darkest dimension of prophetic suffering, and Christ's experience of it is the fulfillment of both Jeremiah's and the psalmist's anticipation.
11: "But the LORD is with me like a mighty and terrible warrior; therefore my persecutors will stumble and not prevail." The divine warrior (gibbôr ʿārîṣ, mighty terrible one) who is Jeremiah's champion is the positive anchor amid the Confession's anguish. Christologically, this confident assertion of divine vindication — in the middle of the deepest prophetic suffering — anticipates Philippians 2:9-11 ("therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name"). The divine warrior who vindicates Jeremiah against his persecutors is the Father who vindicates Christ against death itself. The persecutors who stumble and do not prevail are the precise language of the resurrection's reversal.
12: "O LORD of hosts, who tests the righteous and sees the innermost being — the heart and mind — let me see your vengeance on them, for to you I have committed my case." The appeal to YHWH as the one who sees the klāyôt wālēḇ (kidneys/heart, the innermost self) is the appeal to the divine judge who sees what human courts cannot. Christologically, Revelation 2:23 attributes this same omniscient inner-seeing to the risen Christ: "I am he who searches mind and heart" — Jesus takes YHWH's prerogative of omniscient judgment as his own. The case committed to YHWH in Jeremiah 20:12 is the case committed to the Father by Christ in Luke 23:46 ("Father, into your hands I commit my spirit").
13: "Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD! For he has rescued the life of the poor from the hand of evildoers." The sudden eruption of praise — after the depths of vv7-12 — is the structural pattern of the Individual Lament psalms (Ps 22: anguish → confidence → praise; Ps 69: complaint → trust → praise). Christologically, this movement from dereliction to praise is the passion-resurrection pattern: the cry of abandonment (v7 = Matthew 27:46) gives way to the praise of vindication (Hebrews 2:12: "I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise" — citing Psalms 22:22, itself the praise-section of the lament that begins with the cry of dereliction). The "rescued the life of the poor" in v13 anticipates Acts 2:32 ("God raised this Jesus").
14: "Cursed be the day I was born!" The sudden reversal from praise (v13) to self-curse (vv14-18) is the most psychologically stark transition in Jeremiah. The birth-curse parallels Job 3:3-10 (Job's identical curse of his birth-day). Christologically, the wish that one had never been born — that the incarnation/birth had never happened — is the OT extreme of prophetic despair. But Christ's response to his own suffering is the exact inverse of the birth-curse: in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:39) he says "not as I will, but as you will" — accepting the cup rather than cursing the day. The birth-curse of Jeremiah is the OT extreme that Christ's Gethsemane prayer refuses.
15: "Cursed be the man who brought the news to my father — 'A son has been born to you!' — and made him so happy." The imprecation extends to the messenger of birth-joy — the very announcement of the prophet's birth is now cursed. Christologically, the contrast with the annunciations of the NT is total: the angel announces Jesus's birth with "Fear not, for behold, I bring you good news of great joy" (Luke 2:10) — precisely the birth-joy that Jeremiah curses. The cursed birth-announcement in Jeremiah is the OT shadow of the blessed birth-announcement in Luke 2. Christ's birth inaugurates the joy that Jeremiah's birth led only to suffering.
16: "Let that man be like the cities the LORD destroyed without pity; let him hear a cry of distress in the morning and a battle alarm at noon." The curse invokes the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah ("the cities the LORD destroyed without pity"). Christologically, Jesus himself uses Sodom as the standard of judgment (Matthew 10:15; Jeremiah 11:23-24) — the most extreme OT image of divine wrath without pity. The fact that Jeremiah appeals to Sodom-level destruction as the standard for his personal distress measures the depths of his prophetic suffering.
17: "Because he did not kill me in the womb, so that my mother would have been my grave." The wish for pre-birth death — that the womb had been a tomb — is the ultimate withdrawal of consent from the covenant mission. This is the anti-life cry that stands as the OT's darkest moment of prophetic despair. Christologically, it is also the most precise foil for the incarnation: Christ entered the womb voluntarily (Hebrews 10:5-7: "a body you have prepared for me... I have come to do your will, O God"). Where Jeremiah wishes the womb had been his tomb, Christ accepted the womb as his path to the tomb — and through the tomb to resurrection.
18: "Why did I ever come out of the womb? All I have known is trouble and sorrow, and my life will end in disgrace." The closing cry of the Sixth Confession — unanswered, unresolved, without a restoration-word — is the most open-ended of the Confessions. It does not end in praise (contrast v13). Christologically, the unanswered cry is the OT type of Holy Saturday — the silence between the cry of dereliction (Good Friday) and the resurrection announcement (Easter Sunday). The Confessions' refusal to resolve this cry on its own terms is itself the theological statement that only the resurrection can answer what v18 asks.
